logclip(elivi, ϵ) · ˆAi.
14


--- Page 15 ---
Figure 4: Per-token and total FiberPO objective under parameterized drift ri = 1 + t ˆ∆i (T=10, k=32, ϵ=0.04,
C+=0.12, C−=0.05), with sign( ˆ∆i) = sign( ˆAi), reflecting the typical policy update in which tokens with positive
advantage increase their probability ( ˆ∆i > 0) and tokens with negative advantage decrease their probability ( ˆ∆i < 0).
GRPO in the graph uses the same ϵ-clip as FiberPO.
(a) Trajectory in (log s+, log s−)-space overlaid on the log w heatmap; as drift increases, the trajectory moves through
the global regimes of Figure 2(b). (b) Per-token fiber correction at t=1: fiber residual (red) and base-weight contribution
(orange) stack to form the full gated log-ratio. (c) Per-token objective for two representative tokens. (d) Total objective:
FiberPO’s gagg and logclip saturate, causing the objective to return to baseline. Vertical markers: ϵ-clip transitions
(gray, L-I→L-II), C−rollback (red, G-II,r onset), C+ rollback (blue, G-II,r onset).
Figure 4 traces the per-token and total objective under varying policy drift. The key features are:
• Local regulation engages first (gray vertical lines, panels c, d): individual tokens hit the ϵ-clip boundary
(|ui| = ϵ, L-I→L-II transition) well before the trajectory-level aggregates reach the budget threshold. At this
stage wbase is in G-I, so only per-token spikes are capped while the trajectory-level importance-sampling
correction remains intact.
• Rollback activates on aggregate drift (between solid and dotted red/blue vertical lines): once log s−
τ exceeds
C−(or log s+
τ exceeds C+), gagg enters rollback regime. The base weight wbase
τ
begins to oppose the drift
direction, producing a restorative gradient (G-II,r).
15


--- Page 16 ---
• Non-monotonic pattern (panel d): the total objective rises, peaks as | log wbase
τ
| reaches its maximum (G-
II,r), then declines through mutual rollback (G-III,r) and collapses to the no-reweighting baseline when both
channels are fully gated (G-III, wbase
τ
= 1).
5.4
Theoretical Analysis
Having defined the FiberPO objective and its two-scale gating structure, we now analyze the gradient-level properties
that distinguish it from existing proximal methods.
Gradient Decomposition via the Ratio Gating Jacobian.
The gradient of a generic RGF objective (Definition 3.1),
and thus of FiberPO, with respect to the model parameters θ admits the decomposition:
∂ˆJ(θ|θold)
∂θ
=
X
k∈E
∂zk
∂θ
∂ˆJ
∂zk
=
X
i,j,k∈E
∂zk
∂θ µi
| {z }
parametrization-related
prior weights
∂G(r•)i
∂rj
|
{z
}
J(G)ij
(rj(δjk −(πθ)k))
|
{z
}
Πjk =
∂rj
∂zk
|
{z
}
ratio gating factor
ˆAψ(i)
| {z }
advantage force
,
(19)
where zi is the logit parameter of the policy, i.e., πθ(·|s) = softmax(z(θ, s)) (see Appendix A.4), δjk is the Kronecker
delta, (πθ)k ≡πθ(ak|sk) is the policy probability at the state-action pair indexed by k ∈E, and ψ(i) maps an
augmented index i ∈E to its underlying state-action pair (s, a).
The gradient decomposes into three factors: the parametrization-related prior weights ∂zk
∂θ µi, which are fixed a priori;
the advantage force ˆAψ(i), which comes from the reward model and is also given; and the ratio gating factor, which
is the only component determined by the choice of surrogate objective. Within the ratio gating factor, the matrix
Π ≡

rj(δjk −(πθ)k)

j,k∈E is simply the transformation matrix from logit space to ratio space [ ∂rj
∂zk ]. Therefore,
the Jacobian of the ratio gating map, J(G)ij ≡∂G(r•)i
∂rj
, is the central object that determines how different surrogate
objectives shape the policy update.
The FiberPO Ratio Gating Jacobian.
To characterize how this two-scale gating shapes gradient flow, we compute
the Jacobian of the ratio transform G. Since Gi = exp(log Gi), the Jacobian J(G)ij := ∂G(r•)i
∂rj
factors as Gi
rj · ∂log Gi
∂log rj .
Using the gating decomposition (7) and differentiating through gagg, logclip, and the aggregate ratios s±
τ , one obtains:
Proposition 5.2 (Jacobian of FiberPO ratio transform). The Jacobian of the FiberPO ratio gating map is:
J(G)ij = G(r•)i
rj
Itji=tjj

I|ui|≤ϵ Ii=j
|
{z
}
(a) local self-gating
+
1
Ttji

γ(lj)
tji −I|ui|≤ϵ Ili=lj −I|vi|≤ϵ (1 −Ili=lj)

|
{z
}
(b) trajectory-mediated coupling

,
(20)
where:
• tji represents the trajectory which state-action pair in i belongs to,
• ui := li log ri −log s(li)
tji is the fiber residual of token i (Eq. 14),
• vi := −log s(−li)
tji
is the opposite-sign aggregate (Eq. 15),
• γ(l)
τ
:= (gagg)′(log s(l)
τ , C(l), Tτ) is the derivative of the base gate (Eq. 10), taking the value 1 in the
pass-through regime (| log s(l)
τ | ≤C(l)), −Tτ in the rollback regime (C(l) < | log s(l)
τ | < (1 + T −1
τ
)C(l)), and
0 in the zeroed regime (| log s(l)
τ | ≥(1 + T −1
τ
)C(l)).
Three structural properties follow from this expression.
Property 5.3 (Trajectory independence). The factor Itji=tjj ensures that tokens in different trajectories are fully
decoupled: J(G)ij = 0 whenever tji ̸= tjj. Equivalently, the Jacobian is block-diagonal over trajectories, so each
trajectory’s gradient is self-contained.4
4It is still worth mention that there might still be cross-trajectory coupling via the parametrization-related prior weights ∂zk
∂θ in the
full gradient decomposition (Eq. 19), however, (i) a canonical choice of parametrization should have different parameters controlling
16


--- Page 17 ---
Property 5.4 (First-order agreement). At the on-policy point (r• = 1), the Jacobian reduces to J(G)ij = Ii=j/rj, the
identity gating. The FiberPO surrogate therefore recovers the linear surrogate J(1)(θ|θold) and the true RL objective to
first order near on-policy (Theorem 4.5).
Property 5.5 (Scale separation). The local self-gating term (a) in Eq. 20 has O(1) magnitude. The coupling term (b) is
weighted by 1/Tτ, so trajectory-mediated effects scale inversely with sequence length. In the near-on-policy regime,
the local gradient dominates; trajectory-level corrections become significant only as aggregate drift grows.
6
Fibration Hierarchy and FiberPO-Domain
The fiber bundle model of Section 4.1 captures a single level of abstraction: tokens are grouped into trajectories, and
Fiber Bundle Gating decomposes stability control into a base gate (trajectory level) and a fiber gate (token level). In
practice, however, dependency and classification in LLM RL training are inherently hierarchical. Tokens belong to
trajectories, trajectories belong to prompt groups, and prompt groups belong to domains such as mathematics, code
generation, and instruction following. There is no reason to restrict the fibration to a single level, and the algebraic
structure of FBG extends naturally to a chain of fibrations.
This generalization is not merely a matter of symbolic generality. Different levels of the hierarchy demand different
trust-region radii for substantive reasons. First, different domains may be trained at different stages and therefore have
different proximity to the optimum, requiring appropriately sized per-domain trust regions (a radius that is too large
causes instability, while one that is too small leads to premature convergence to a local optimum). Second, parameters
associated with each hierarchical level carry different degrees of importance in the network architecture and should
accordingly be given different amounts of flexibility. Third, even the positive and negative drift channels within each
level demand different budgets, to avoid overly optimistic updates from accidental rewards or excessive punishment
of exploratory behavior. Existing methods provide only coarse control over these demands: GRPO applies a uniform
per-token clip, GSPO operates at a single trajectory level, and APC-Obj and TRPO enforce a global aggregate trust
region that does not discriminate across finer structures. FiberPO-Trajectory (Section 5) improves on these by coupling
trajectory-level and token-level control, but it operates at only two levels of the hierarchy. In this section, we show that
the fibration formalism generalizes naturally to arbitrarily many hierarchical levels, providing a principled compositional
framework for multi-scale trust-region control. We formalize this generalization as the Uniform Fibration Hierarchy
and its associated Fibration Gating Hierarchy, and demonstrate its utility by deriving FiberPO-Domain, a concrete
four-level instantiation (domain, prompt group, trajectory, token). The same construction extends readily to any level of
hierarchical complexity that a given training setup requires.
6.1
Uniform Fibration Hierarchy and Fibration Gating Hierarchy
We begin by extending the fiber bundle model to a chain of fibrations, each representing one level of abstraction.
Definition 6.1 (Uniform Fibration Hierarchy). A uniform fibration hierarchy of depth n is a chain of spaces
{Bk}k∈{0,...,n} (called strata) together with fibrations {πk}k∈{1,...,n}, where each πk : Bk →Bk−1 is a fibration
projection:
B0
π1
←−B1
π2
←−B2
π3
←−· · ·
πn
←−Bn ≡E.
(21)
The chain of fibrations naturally induces a chain of pushforward maps on density spaces:
B0
π1∗
←−B1
π2∗
←−B2
π3∗
←−· · ·
πn∗
←−Bn ≡E,
(22)
where Bk denotes the space of densities on Bk.
Building on the uniform fibration hierarchy, we equip each stratum with gating infrastructure analogous to Fiber Bundle
Gating (Definition 4.3).
Definition 6.2 (Fibration Gating Hierarchy (FGH)). A Fibration Gating Hierarchy consists of the following objects:
Object 1. Uniform fibration hierarchy {Bk, πk} (Definition 6.1).
Object 2. Reflecting Markov kernels {Kk}k∈{0,...,n−1}, Kk : Bk →Bk+1:
B0
K0
−→B1
K1
−→B2
K2
−→· · ·
Kn−1
−→Bn ≡E.
for unrelated trajectories, thus typically leading to sparse ∂zk
∂θ and thus minimal unrelated cross-trajectory coupling. In contrast, the
related trajectory-level coupling of parametrization is in fact desirable, as it allows more stable and robust gradient direction provided
by correlated gradients of related trajectories. (ii) The parametrization-related prior weights ∂zk
∂θ are independent of the choice of
surrogate objective and thus is not possible to further control with surrogate formulations. The block-diagonal Jacobian already
provides maximal decoupling that can be achieved at the surrogate level.
17


--- Page 18 ---
Object 3. Atomic gating functions on each stratum, {gk,p<k}k∈{0,...,n}:
gk,p<k : Bk →Bk,
where gk,p<k may depend on the densities on all lower strata p<k ≡(p0, p1, . . . , pk−1).
Object 4. Fibration decomposition and recovery maps, the same as in FBG (Definition 4.3): F : RE →E,
R :
E →RE.
These objects are subject to the following conditions:
(i) Reflecting condition: πk∗◦Kk−1 = idBk−1 for 0 < k ≤n, equivalently πk∗(σ −Kk−1(πk∗σ)) = 0,
ensuring that the residual at each level carries no lower-stratum information.
(ii) Recovery condition: R ◦F = idRE.
(iii) Locality condition: gk,p<k = L
b∈Bk gk,p<k,b, i.e., gk,p<k(σk)b = gk,p<k,b((σk)b) for all σk ∈Bk and
b ∈Bk. Each atomic gating function acts pointwise over the elements of its stratum.
For notational convenience, we define sequential compositions of the fibration and kernel maps. Let
Km←k := Km−1 ◦Km−2 ◦· · · ◦Kk+1 ◦Kk
for k < m,
Kk←k := idBk,
(23)
πk←m := πk+1 ◦πk+2 ◦· · · ◦πm−1 ◦πm
for k < m,
πk←k := idBk,
(24)
πk←m ∗:= πk+1 ∗◦πk+2 ∗◦· · · ◦πm−1 ∗◦πm∗
for k < m,
πk←k ∗:= idBk.
(25)
We also write π<k ∗σ ≡(π0←n ∗σ, π1←n ∗σ, . . . , πk−1←n ∗σ) for σ ∈E ≡Bn, collecting the pushed-forward
densities on all strata below level k.
The FGH gating function generalizes the FBG operator (4) by gating the residual at each stratum and reflecting all
contributions back to the total space:
G(σ) := Kn←0 ◦g0 ◦π0←n ∗(σ) +
X
0<k≤n
Kn←k ◦gk,π<k ∗σ(πk←n ∗σ −Kk−1(πk−1←n ∗σ)) ,
(26)
which admits the compact form
G(σ) =
X
0≤k≤n
Kn←k ◦Gk ◦πk←n ∗(σ),
(27)
where the per-stratum operator Gk is defined by
Gk(σk) :=
(
g0(σk)
if k = 0,
gk,π<k ∗σ(σk −Kk−1 ◦πk∗(σk))
otherwise.
(28)
The structure mirrors that of Fiber Bundle Gating: the density σ is pushed forward to each stratum Bk via πk←n ∗, the
contribution already accounted for at stratum k−1 is subtracted through σk −Kk−1 ◦πk∗(σk), the residual is gated by
gk,π<k ∗σ, and all gated components are reflected back to E via Kn←k and summed.
The associated FGH surrogate objective is defined analogously to the FBG gating form (5):
ˆJFGH(θ|θold) =
X
i∈E
µi R ◦G ◦F(r•(θ|θold))i ˆAi.
(29)
Remark 6.3 (Recovery of Fiber Bundle Gating). When n = 1, the fibration hierarchy reduces to a single fiber bundle
π1 : B1 ≡E →B0 ≡B, and the FGH definitions recover exactly the FBG framework of Definition 4.3. In this
case, the gating function (26) reduces to G(σ) = K0(g0(π1∗σ)) + g1,π1∗σ(σ −K0(π1∗σ)), which is precisely (4). The
FiberPO objective (Definition 5.1), which operates at the trajectory and token levels, corresponds to n = 1.
6.2
FiberPO-Domain
We now instantiate the Fibration Gating Hierarchy with n = 3 to obtain FiberPO-Domain, which extends FiberPO’s
two-level gating (trajectory, token) to a four-level hierarchy: domain, prompt group, trajectory, and token.
We first introduce the relevant notation. Let D ∈Domain denote a domain index (e.g., mathematics, code, instruction
following), and let Dg denote the domain of prompt group g. For simplicity, we assume that training prompts are not
18


--- Page 19 ---
composite domain tasks, so that we may also write Dτ for the domain of trajectory τ and Di := Dτ for the domain
of an augmented state-action pair i ≡(s, a, τ, t) ∈¯
X. The prompt group index of trajectory τ is denoted gτ, and
gi denotes the prompt group of augmented pair i. Let τi denote the trajectory to which i belongs, Tτ the length of
trajectory τ, TD := P
τ∈D Tτ the total number of state-action pairs classified to domain D, and Tg := P
τ∈g Tτ the
total number of state-action pairs within prompt group g. We write Tjθold(D) for the set of trajectories in domain D
and Tjθold(g) for those in prompt group g.
Definition 6.4 (FiberPO-Domain). The FiberPO-Domain objective is:
ˆJFiberPO-Domain(θ|θold) =
X
i∈¯
X
1
|Tjθold|
1
Tτ
wBase
i
· ˜rFiber
i
· ˆAi.
(30)
The base weight decomposes into six gated aggregate terms, three per sign channel, corresponding to the domain,
prompt group, and trajectory levels:
wBase
i
:= exp
 
gagg(log s+
Di, C+, TDi) + gagg(log s+
gi −log s+
Di, C+, Tgi) + gagg(log s+
τi −log s+
gi, C+, Tτi)
−
gagg(log s−
Di, C−, TDi) −gagg(log s−
gi −log s−
Di, C−, Tgi) −gagg(log s−
τi −log s−
gi, C−, Tτi)
!
,
(31)
where the aggregate gate gagg is defined in Eq. 10. The gated residual is identical to FiberPO’s fiber gate (Eq. 11):
˜rFiber
i
:= logclip
␀(s(li)
τi )−li ri, ϵ

logclip
␀(s(−li)
τi
)−li, ϵ
 ,
(32)
where li := sign(log ri). The prompt-group-level and domain-level aggregate log-ratios appearing in the base weight
are defined as:
log s(l)
g
:=
1
|Tjθold(g)|
X
τ∈g
log s(l)
τ ,
log s(l)
D :=
X
g∈D
|Tjθold(g)|
|Tjθold(D)|
log s(l)
g .
(33)
The associated FGH objects (strata, reflecting kernels, atomic gates, and decomposition/recovery maps) that realize this
objective as an n=3 FGH instance are specified in Appendix E.7.
The base weight (31) has a transparent hierarchical structure. Each of the three positive terms gates the residual drift at
one level of the hierarchy: log s+
Di captures domain-level drift, log s+
gi −log s+
Di captures the prompt group’s deviation
from its domain aggregate, and log s+
τi −log s+
gi captures the trajectory’s deviation from its prompt group aggregate.
The negative channel is treated symmetrically. At each level, gagg independently maintains a trust-region budget with
the same three-regime (pass-through, rollback, zeroed) behavior as in FiberPO-Trajectory (Section 5.1), scaled by the
appropriate count (TDi, Tgi, or Tτi).
FiberPO-Domain inherits the structural merits that FiberPO-Trajectory demonstrates at the trajectory and token levels.
The first-order agreement with the true RL objective near on-policy extends to the full hierarchy under the same
identity conditions on the atomic gates (Theorem 4.5 applied to the FGH framework). The ratio decoupling property
(Section 5.2), which ensures that tokens are regulated by their deviation from the trajectory mean rather than by their
absolute log-ratio, now extends upward: each trajectory is regulated by its deviation from the prompt group aggregate,
and each prompt group is regulated by its deviation from the domain aggregate. The rollback mechanism operates
independently at each level, so that budget violation at one level of the hierarchy does not contaminate the gating at
other levels. This information decoupling between hierarchical levels provides substantially more versatile optimization
control for multi-domain LLM RL training than any single-scale or two-scale method.
The FiberPO-Domain objective (30) admits an equivalent FGH gating form G(r•)i = wBase
i
· ˜rFiber
i
= R ◦G ◦F(r•)i
corresponding to the n = 3 case of Definition 6.2. The detailed specification of the constituent FGH objects
(fibration hierarchy, reflecting Markov kernels, atomic gating functions, and decomposition/recovery maps) is given in
Appendix E.7.
Remark 6.5 (Heterogeneous fibration systems). The uniform fibration hierarchy assumes a linear chain of strata
B0 ←B1 ←· · · ←Bn, which suffices for the domain–prompt-group–trajectory–token hierarchy considered here.
A more general construction replaces the linear chain with a rooted tree (or more generally, a quiver), where each
edge carries a fibration and each vertex carries a gating function. Given a rooted tree with source and target maps
s, t : Edge →Vertex (edges directed toward the root), one assigns a space Fv to each vertex and a fibration
F1(e) : Fs(e) →Ft(e) to each edge, together with reflecting kernels and atomic gates at each vertex. The resulting
gating operator aggregates gated residuals from all vertices back to the total leaf space, generalizing the linear sum (26)
to a tree-indexed sum. This heterogeneous fibration system accommodates branching hierarchies (e.g., separate domain
and modality classifications at the same level) and provides a framework for expressing gating algebras over arbitrary
dependency structures.
19


--- Page 20 ---
7
Conclusion
This paper develops an algebraic approach to multi-scale stability control in reinforcement learning for large language
models. We begin by proving that classical TRPO trust-region guarantees collapse at discount factor γ = 1, the
regime required by sparse-reward LLM tasks, motivating a separation of the trust-region maintenance mechanism
from the specific radius prescribed by the classical bound. We then derive Aggregational Policy Censoring Objective
(APC-Obj), an unconstrained clipping-based surrogate that is provably equivalent to sample-based TV-TRPO under
complete parametrization, establishing that clipping-based surrogate design and trust-region policy optimization are
dual formulations of the same problem. Through the Ratio Gating Formalism (RGF), we show that PPO, GRPO, and
GSPO each arise from APC-Obj via identifiable relaxation steps, making each method’s departure from the trust-region
optimum explicit.
Building on this analytical foundation, we introduce Fiber Bundle Gating (FBG), a framework that organizes sampled
RLHF data as a fiber bundle and decomposes ratio gating into coordinated global and local components operating on
densities over the base and total spaces. FBG preserves first-order agreement with the true RL objective near on-policy
whenever the atomic gates reduce to identity at the reference point. From a relaxed APC-Obj formulation, we derive
FiberPO, a concrete FBG instantiation whose trajectory-level aggregate gate and token-level residual gate provide
independent trust-region budgets at two scales. The FiberPO Jacobian is block-diagonal over trajectories, reduces
to identity at on-policy, and exhibits a restorative gradient structure in the rollback regime that is absent in all prior
methods.
To our knowledge, this is the first work to provide a principled algebraic framework that unifies trust-region maintenance,
multi-scale gating, and gradient-level guarantees within a single coherent formalism. A natural question is whether
the fiber bundle apparatus is necessary, or whether the same multi-scale gating could be achieved with a carefully
hand-crafted structured clipping rule. We observe that methods coupling global and local stability control necessarily
involves a dependency structure in which many local quantities depend on a shared global context. This dependency
defines a fibration πE : E →B, and the three objects of a fiber bundle, the total space E, the base space B, and
the projection πE, correspond directly to the components already present in the data. Methods that aggregate local
quantities into context-level statistics implicitly invoke the pushforward πE∗, and methods that distribute context-level
information back to local entries implicitly construct a Markov kernel K. The formalism makes these operations explicit
and thereby exposes the reflecting condition πE∗◦K = idB (for methods coupling global and local information to
satisfy), a structural constraint ensuring that the residual σ−K(πE∗σ) carries no base-level information (Appendix E.3).
Without this condition, the fiber gate re-gates global information already handled by the base gate, or the base gate
inadvertently constrains genuinely local variation. Designing a gating map that avoids this double-counting while
preserving first-order agreement (Theorem 4.5) amounts to satisfying exactly the reflecting condition, i.e., implicitly
re-deriving the fiber bundle decomposition. The formalism identifies and makes explicit an underlying decomposition
structure that multi-scale gating has, and provides a principled framework for reasoning about its correctness.
Because fibrations compose algebraically, the same three-object template chains into the Fibration Gating Hierarchy
(FGH) without introducing new primitives, with each stratum’s residual capturing the purest local variation at that level
after all coarser influence has been removed. FiberPO-Domain (domain, prompt group, trajectory, token) is one such
instantiation, and deeper hierarchies follow by appending another fibration layer. Achieving the same compositional
guarantee with ad-hoc notation would require re-proving orthogonality and first-order agreement at every new level.
The algebraic compositionality of fibrations provides this automatically. This positions FBG as a foundation for the
stability challenges that arise in large-scale agentic systems and heterogeneous multi-domain training.
20


--- Page 21 ---
A
Notation and Preliminaries
A.1
On MDP
For a given MDP, we denote its action space A, state space S, state transition probability T (s′|s, a), its θ-parameter
dependent policy πθ(a|s), and reward function R : S →R.
We denote the space of all signed measures over a space X (equipped with a sigma algebra on it) as M(X). The space
of probability measures is denoted P(X), and the space of smooth probability measures is denoted C∞P(X) when X
is equipped with a smooth structure.
We denote Pp0,πθ,T (or simply Pθ if T and p0 are clear or assumed from context) as the probabilistic distribution of RL
trajectories given the prior distribution p0 for initial states, the πθ action policy, and the state transition distribution T .
The discounted RL objective is denoted
J(θ) := Eτ∼Pθ
h ∞
X
t=0
γtR(st(τ))
i
.
Given a reference policy parameter θold, we define:
Policy-likelihood ratio.
The policy-likelihood ratio (or policy ratio) is defined to be
rs,a(θ|θold) :=
πθ(a|s)
πθold(a|s),
a ∈A, s ∈S.
If θ, θold are clear from context, we abbreviate this as rs,a.
Linear surrogate RL objective.
The linear surrogate RL objective (with respect to θold) is denoted
J(1)(θ|θold) := J(θold) + Eτ∼Pθold
h ∞
X
t=0
γtrst(τ),at(τ)(θ|θold) A(θold)
st(τ),at(τ)
i
,
where A(θold)
s,a
is the advantage value for the state-action pair (s, a) given the reference policy πθold.
The linear surrogate RL objective is the first-order approximation to the true RL objective near on-policy (θ ≈θold),
and thus has the same gradient as the true RL objective at θ = θold [8]:
∇θJ(1)(θ|θold)

θ=θold = ∇θJ(θ)

θ=θold.
Advantage norm.
We further denote max(s,a)∈S×A |A(θ)
s,a| ≡∥A(θ)
• ∥∞, the L∞norm of the advantage A(θ)
•
(for
placeholder • running over all state-action pairs), a notation agreeing with our later convention.
Discounted state distribution.
We denote the discounted state distribution ρπθ
(γ), defined as
ρπθ
(γ)(US) := Eτ∼Pθ
h ∞
X
t=0
γtIUS( st(τ) )
i
,
where US ⊆S is a measurable set in state space S, and IX(x) :=

1
if x ∈X
0
otherwise
is the indicator function. Note
that for LLM γ = 1, this infinite sum does not necessarily converge. However, typical LLMs have finite response length
(or at least clip overly long responses), so the discounted state distribution can still be defined.
Normalized state distribution.
We define the normalized state distribution
¯ρπθ :=

where Tτ is the length of trajectory τ.

A.2
On Estimation for MDP
In the context of estimation, we denote ˆEX∼p[X] the Monte Carlo estimation of EX∼p[X].
For trajectory distributions Pθ, we denote Tjθ the collection of all sampled trajectories, Tjθ(s0) the set of all sampled
trajectories from initial state s0, and X the collection of all state-action pairs that appeared in sampled trajectories, i.e.,
X := {(st(τ), at(τ)) | τ ∈Tjθ},
in this given context of estimation. For later convenience, we also denote s0[Tjθ] the set of all initial states s0 that
appeared in the sampled trajectories.
We denote the augmented (sampled) state-action pair space
¯
X := {(st(τ), at(τ), τ, t)} ⊆X × Tjπθold × N
(for mathematical completeness, even if a trajectory terminates at a finite step, we still include the null state-action pair
(∅, ∅, τ, t) after termination).
For any state-action pair (s, a), we define ns,a ∈N as the number of times (s, a) appears in all the sampled trajectories
Tjθ (counting multiplicity inside the same trajectory as well). We further denote the normalized counting measure on
space X as µX :
µX ({(s, a)}) ≡µs,a =
ns,a
P
(s′,a′)∈X ns′,a′ .
We also introduce, for a trajectory τ, the quantity n(τ)
s,a representing the number of times state-action pair (s, a) appears
in trajectory τ. (For LLM, it never goes beyond 1. However, for rigorousness and generality to RL, we still employ this
notation; furthermore, we always assign n∅,∅= 0 for the null state-action pair.) The normalized counting measure
µ(τ)
X
is associated to it:
µ(τ)
X ({(s, a)}) ≡µ(τ)
s,a =
n(τ)
s,a
P
(s′,a′)∈X (τ) n(τ)
s′,a′
,
where X (τ) is the set of all state-action pairs that appear in trajectory τ.
We also denote µ(τ)
¯
X
:=
1
Tτ I∈τ the normalized counting measure on trajectory τ over the augmented state-action space
¯
X, where
I∈τ((s, a, τ ′, t)) =

1
if τ = τ ′
0
otherwise ,
and we conveniently denote ¯µ(τ)
s,a,t ≡¯µ(s,a,τ,t) ≡µ(τ)
¯
X ({(s, a, τ, t)}).
For convenience, we also define the normalized counting measure on natural numbers N (and subsets thereof) with
respect to a trajectory as µ(τ) ∈P(N), µ(τ)({n}) :=
1
Tτ I[0,Tτ )(n).
A.3
On Divergences and Their Monte-Carlo Estimation
We define the total variation (TV) distance and Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence at three levels—per-state, average/max
over states, and trajectory-normalized—and give the distributional definition, the equivalent ratio form, and the
Monte-Carlo estimator for each. All divergences are measured from πθold to πθ using the policy ratio rs,a :=
πθ(a|s)/πθold(a|s); we abbreviate D(θold∥θ) when the policies are clear from context.
A.3.1
Per-State Divergences
Definitions.
For a fixed state s ∈S, the per-state TV distance and KL divergence between πθold(·|s) and πθ(·|s) are
DTV(s) := DTV
␀πθold(·|s)
 πθ(·|s)

= 1
2
X
a∈A
πθold(a|s) −πθ(a|s)
,
(34)
DKL(s) := DKL
␀πθold(·|s)
 πθ(·|s)

=
X
a∈A
πθold(a|s) log πθold(a|s)
πθ(a|s) .
(35)
They are related by Pinsker’s inequality:
DTV(s)2 ≤
1
2 DKL(s),
(36)
which is used throughout this paper to convert between KL-based and TV-based bounds.
22


--- Page 23 ---
Ratio forms.
Using rs,a = πθ(a|s)/πθold(a|s), the per-state divergences can be rewritten as expectations under
πθold(·|s):
DTV(s) = 1
2 Ea∼πθold(·|s)

|rs,a −1|

,
(37)
DKL(s) = −Ea∼πθold(·|s)

log rs,a

.
(38)
The TV ratio form (37) follows from factoring out πθold(a|s) in (34):
DTV(s) = 1
2
X
a∈A
πθold(a|s) −πθ(a|s)
 = 1
2
X
a∈A
πθold(a|s)

πθold(a|s) −πθ(a|s)
πθold(a|s)

= 1
2
X
a∈A
πθold(a|s)
1 −rs,a
 = 1
2 Ea∼πθold(·|s)

|rs,a −1|

.
The KL ratio form (38) follows from log(πθold/πθ) = −log rs,a in (35).
Monte-Carlo estimation.
Let Xs := {a : (s, a) ∈X} be the set of actions sampled at state s, with multiplicities ns,a
per-state sample count Ts := P
a∈Xs ns,a, total sample count T = P
s Ts is the total sample count, and the augmented
state-action space ¯
X (Appendix A.2).
The Monte-Carlo estimators of the per-state divergences are:
ˆDTV(s) :=
1
Ts
X
a∈Xs
ns,a |rs,a −1|,
(39)
ˆDKL(s) := −1
Ts
X
a∈Xs
ns,a log rs,a,
(40)
which are the empirical means of the ratio-form integrands (37)–(38) over the samples at state s.
A.3.2
Average and Max Divergences
Average divergence.
The average TV and KL divergences weight the per-state quantities by the normalized state
distribution ¯ρπθold (Appendix A.1):
¯DTV/KL(θold∥θ) := Es∼¯ρ
πθold

DTV/KL(s)

.
(41)
Estimation of the average divergence.
The augmented state-action space ¯
X (Appendix A.2) pools tokens across all
trajectories, so that the empirical state frequencies in ¯
X are proportional to ¯ρπθold , i.e., Ts
T ∼¯ρπθold(s) as T →∞. The
average divergence is therefore estimated by weighting the per-state estimators (39)–(40) by their sample counts, which
can be expanded in two equivalent ways—over the states S[X] with multiplicities, or directly over the augmented space
¯
X:
ˆ¯DTV/KL(θold∥θ) = 1
T
X
s∈S[X]
Ts ˆDTV/KL(s) = 1
T
X
s∈S[X]
X
a∈Xs
ns,a f(rs,a) = 1
T
X
(s,a,τ,t)∈¯
X
f(rs,a),
(42)
where f(rs,a) = |rs,a −1| for TV, and respectively f(rs,a) = −log rs,a for KL, and S[X] is the set of states appearing
in X. The second equality substitutes the definition of ˆDTV/KL(s) (Eqs. 39–40); the third unfolds the state-action
multiplicities into the augmented index (s, a, τ, t), since each occurrence of (s, a) in ¯
X contributes exactly one term.
Note that the estimation here is for the undiscounted case. We do not use discounted averages of TV or KL(or their
estimation) elsewhere in this paper; for the generic form of discounted estimators, see Appendix D.1.
Max TV divergence.
The max TV divergence takes the worst-case per-state TV over the entire state space:
Dmax
TV (θold∥θ) := max
s∈S DTV(s).
(43)
This quantity appears in the TRPO surrogate-gap bound (Eq. 1). Its sample-based estimator restricts the maximum to
states observed in the batch:
ˆDmax
TV (θold∥θ) :=
max
s∈S[X]
ˆDTV(s),
(44)
which is used in the sample-based TV-TRPO formulation (Appendix D.2).

--- Page 24 ---
A.3.3
Trajectory-Normalized Divergences
Definition.
The trajectory-normalized TV and KL divergences average the per-state quantities over the trajectory-
level normalized state distribution ¯ρ
πθold
(tj) , which weights each state by its frequency within individual trajectories
(Appendix A.1):
¯D(tj)
TV/KL(θold∥θ) := Es∼¯ρ
πθold
(tj)

DTV/KL(s)

.
(45)
The key distinction from ¯DTV/KL (41) is the state distribution: ¯ρπθold weights states by their overall frequency across the
sample, whereas ¯ρ
πθold
(tj)
gives each trajectory equal weight and distributes that weight uniformly among the trajectory’s
states.
Ratio forms.
Substituting the per-state ratio forms (37)–(38) into (45) and using the law of total expectation yields:
¯D(tj)
TV(θold∥θ) = 1
2 E s∼¯ρ
πθold
(tj)
,
a∼πθold(·|s)

|rs,a −1|

,
¯D(tj)
KL (θold∥θ) = −E s∼¯ρ
πθold
(tj)
,
a∼πθold(·|s)

log rs,a

.
(46)
Estimation.
We describe three related estimations for ¯D(tj)
TV/KL, and ¯DTV/KL.
(i) Single-trajectory estimation via ˆE(τ).
For a fixed trajectory τ ∼Pθold, the sequence of states {st(τ)}Tτ −1
t=0
constitutes samples from ¯ρ
πθold
(tj) , with actions drawn from πθold(·|st(τ)). We define the single-trajectory Monte-Carlo
estimator ˆE(τ) as the empirical average over the state-action pairs visited by trajectory τ:
ˆE(τ)[f(s, a)] :=
1
Tτ
Tτ −1
X
t=0
f
␀rst(τ), at(τ)

,
(47)
where all samples come from the single trajectory τ. Applying this to the per-state divergences:
ˆ¯D(τ)
TV(θold∥θ) := ˆE(τ)
DTV(s)

=
1
Tτ

X
t=0
|rst(τ),at(τ) −1|,
(48)
ˆ¯D(τ)

DKL(s)

= −1
Tτ

X
t=0
log rst(τ),at(τ).
(49)
Averaging the single-trajectory estimators over trajectories recovers the population quantity:
¯D(tj)
TV/KL(θold∥θ) = Eτ∼Pθold
 ˆ¯D(τ)
TV/KL(θold∥θ)

= E(s,a)∼¯ρ
πθold
(tj)
·πθold

f(rs,a)

.
(50)
It is worth mentioning that the single-trajectory estimator ˆ¯D(τ)
TV/KL is not a single-sample estimator of the trajectory-
normalized divergence ¯D(tj)
TV/KL. We have mentioned the sequence of states(resp. state-action pairs) from a single
trajectory τ are many samples from the trajectory-length-normalized state distribution ¯ρ
πθold
(tj)
(resp. ¯ρ
πθold
(tj)
· πθold), so
the single-trajectory estimator ˆ¯D(τ)
TV/KL is an empirical average over those samples, and thus is a more accurate many
sample(Tτ many samples) Monte-Carlo estimator of ¯D(tj)
TV/KL according to the second equality.
(ii) Estimation over the augmented state-action space. Equivalently, ¯D(tj)
TV/KL can be estimated by averaging over the
full augmented state-action space ¯
X(which contains not just one, but many trajectories), but weighting each sample by
the inverse length of the trajectory it belongs to:
ˆ¯D(tj)
TV/KL(θold∥θ) =
1
|Tjθold|
X
(s,a,τ,t)∈¯
X
1
Tτ
f(rs,a),
(51)
where f(rs,a) = |rs,a−1| for TV (resp. −log rs,a for KL), and |Tjθold| is the number of sampled trajectories. The factor
1/Tτ ensures that each trajectory contributes equally regardless of its length, matching the trajectory-length-normalized
state distribution ¯ρ
πθold
(tj) .
24


--- Page 25 ---
(iii) Recovering the average divergence from trajectory estimates. Given a set of sampled trajectories Tjθold, weighting
each single-trajectory estimate by its trajectory length recovers the (non-trajectory-normalized) average divergence (41):
ˆ¯DTV/KL(θold∥θ) = 1
T
X
τ∈Tjθold
Tτ ˆ¯D(τ)
TV/KL(θold∥θ) = 1
T
X
(s,a,τ,t)∈¯
X
f(rs,a),
(52)
where T = P
τ Tτ is the total sample count. This follows because the trajectory-level estimator ˆ¯D(τ)
TV/KL averages
uniformly over Tτ tokens within trajectory τ, so multiplying by Tτ/T reweights each token equally across all trajectories,
which is exactly the empirical average over ¯
X and thus estimates ¯DTV/KL under the normalized state distribution
¯ρπθold.
A.4
On Ratio Space
For sets X, Y , we denote Y X the collection of all functions from X to Y .
Definition A.1 (Policy-ratio space). Given the new policy πθ and old policy πθold, we can sample a set of state-action
pairs X. The space RX
≥0 ≡(R≥0)X assigns a non-negative real number to every (s, a) ∈X. We define RX
>0 as the
policy ratio space of all possible policy ratios over the sampled state-action space X. We typically denote its elements
(the tuples of policy ratios) as r•(θ|θold) ≡r• (for placeholder • running over all X). A tuple consists of components:
rst,at(θ|θold) ≡rst,at :=
πθ(at|st)
πθold(at|st).
Remark A.2. To incorporate more information, we sometimes use the space of state-action pairs with extra information
instead of X. For example, in our fiber bundle and FiberPO, we use ¯
X and R ¯
X
>0. In general RGF forms, we use E and
RE
>0.
Subscript conventions.
Similarly, for elements in Y X , we use the bullet subscript (−)• to denote its placeholder
index, which runs over X, and (−)s,a ∈Y is the element evaluated at state-action pair (s, a). We denote |q•|p the Lp
norm of quantity q• ∈CX in the measure space (X, µX ).
Parity superscript convention.
In FiberPO, the sign assignment li := sign(log ri) ∈{+1, −1} partitions tokens into
positive and negative channels. When l (or li, ls,a) appears as a superscript in parentheses, it selects the corresponding
sign channel: s(l)
τ
denotes s+
τ when l = +1 and s−
τ when l = −1, and similarly C(l), γ(l)
τ , etc. The negated form s(−l)
τ
denotes the opposite channel.
Logit parametrization.
We also introduce the quantity z• ∈RX or z•,• ∈RS×A, called the logit parameter, the
output of the second-to-last layer of the neural network before the softmax activation. That is,
πθ(a|s) =
exp(zs,a(θ))
P
a′: (s,a′)∈X exp(zs,a′(θ)).
This is a natural parametrization of probability measures, with one redundant dimension given by adding a constant to
all logits (which maps to the same probability measure).
B
TRPO: Background, Proof, and Discussion
B.1
TV-Based TRPO (TV-TRPO)
Consider the discounted RL objective
J(θ) := Eτ∼Pθ
h ∞
X
t=0
γt Rt(τ)
i
,
(53)
and its linear surrogate with respect to a reference policy πθold:
J(1)(θ | θold) := J(θold) + Eτ∼Pθold
h ∞
X
t=0
γt rst(τ),at(τ)(θ | θold) A(θold)
st(τ),at(τ)
i
,
(54)
25


--- Page 26 ---
where rs,a(θ | θold) := πθ(a | s) / πθold(a | s) is the importance-sampling ratio and A(θold)
s,a
is the advantage under πθold.
The linear surrogate is the first-order approximation of the true objective near on-policy (θ ≈θold); in particular,
∇θJ(1)(θ | θold)

θ=θold = ∇θJ(θ)

θ=θold.
Trust region policy optimization (TRPO) [6] bounds the surrogate gap:
J(θ) −J(1)(θ | θold) ≥−4γ ∥A(θold)
•
∥∞
(1 −γ)2
Dmax
TV (θ ∥θold)2,
(55)
where Dmax
TV (θ ∥θold) := maxs DTV
␀πθold(· | s) ∥πθ(· | s)

.
Note that, unlike the original TRPO formulation of [6] which uses a KL-divergence penalty, here we work with the
total-variation (TV) form throughout, since TV distance is the divergence that arises naturally from the performance-
difference bound above.
TRPO’s monotonic improvement guarantee.
Based on this bound, TRPO constructs a minorization–maximization
(MM) algorithm: at each step, (55) provides a minorizer of the true objective J(θ), and the update maximizes this
minorizer, i.e. θnew is obtained by maximizing the penalized surrogate
J(1)(θ | θold) −4γ ∥A(θold)
•
∥∞
(1 −γ)2
Dmax
TV (θ ∥θold)2.
(56)
Since the true objective is bounded below by (56) and the optimum of (56) is at least J(θold) (attained at θ = θold), the
true objective is guaranteed to be non-decreasing at every update, making the policy optimization stable.
We refer to this MM algorithm as TV-based TRPO or TV-TRPO.
Sample-based TV-TRPO.
In practice, Dmax
TV is intractable to compute over large state spaces, so it is replaced by the
sample maximum
ˆ
max to estimate Dmax
TV , this will be termed as sample-based TV-TRPO objective that serves as the
theoretical foundation for the remainder of this paper.
B.2
Proof of the TRPO Vanishing Theorem
We first introduce the necessary definitions, then state and prove the theorem.
Definition B.1 (Trust Region). For δ ≥0, define the TV trust region as
BTV-TR
δ
(θold) :=

π ∈(S →P(A))
 Dmax
TV (πθold ∥π) ≤δ
	
,
and the KL trust region as
BKL-TR
δ
(θold) :=

π ∈(S →P(A))
 Dmax
KL (πθold ∥π) ≤δ
	
,
where P(A) denotes the space of probability measures over the action space.
The surrogate-gap bound (55) implies that TRPO updates are confined to a trust region whose radius depends on γ:
Lemma B.2 (TV-TRPO update lies in the trust region). Define the TRPO trust-region radius δ(TRPO) := 1−γ
8γ (see
also (76)). For any θnew produced by sample-based TV-TRPO, πθnew ∈BTV-TR
δ(TRPO)(θold) (This is a restatement of
Lemma D.12).
Theorem B.3 (TRPO vanishing theorem; restatement of Theorem 2.1). When γ = 1, both the TV-based and KL-based
TRPO trust regions collapse to the reference policy:
BTV-TR
δ(TRPO)(θold) = BKL-TR
δ(TRPO)(θold) = { πθold }
Proof. By Lemma B.2, taking γ →1−in δ(TRPO) = 1−γ
8γ yields δ(TRPO) →0+; at γ = 1 we have δ(TRPO) = 0.
Any policy πθ inside the trust region must therefore satisfy Dmax
TV (πθold ∥π) = 0 (and likewise for KL). This implies
πθold(· | s) = πθ(· | s) for every s, i.e., πθ is identical to πθold.
26


--- Page 27 ---
B.3
On γ-Annealing as a Workaround
One might attempt to circumvent the vanishing theorem by introducing a variable discount factor γ < 1 into LLM RL,
applying TRPO during training, and gradually annealing γ →1. Similar discount-scheduling strategies have been
explored in other RL settings [9].
However, this approach faces a fundamental practical obstacle in LLM RL. Language model reward functions are
typically sparse and discontinuous: they yield a meaningful signal only at the end of a response, and often in an
all-or-nothing fashion—the response is either fully correct or receives no reward. Some approaches incorporate a partial,
continuous reward signal via a KL divergence penalty term [1], but this remains the exception rather than the rule in
RLVR settings.
Introducing a variable discount factor γ < 1 into such reward landscapes would exponentially suppress later tokens in
the training objective J(θ) = E[P
t γtRt], systematically downweighting the very signals (end-of-response correctness)
that RL is meant to optimize. Moreover, the interaction between a changing γ and the already-discontinuous reward
surface can further destabilize reward estimation, as the effective reward scale shifts across training. These difficulties
make a γ-annealing strategy impractical for LLM RL, reinforcing the need for a direct approach to trust-region-style
stabilization in the γ = 1 case.
C
RGF and APC-Obj: Derivations
C.1
APC-Obj in RGF form
Definition C.1 (APC-Obj policy iteration). The APC-Obj update selects θnew = arg maxθ ˆJAPC-Obj(θ|θold), where
ˆJAPC-Obj(θ|θold) = 1
T
X
(s,a,τ,t)∈¯
X

clip

rs,a −1, Tsδ(APC-Obj) −
X
(s,a′,τ ′,t′)∈¯
X
(a′,τ ′,t′)̸=(a,τ,t)
|rs,a′ −1|

ˆAθold
s,a + ˆAθold
s,a

,
(57)
where δ(APC-Obj) :=
(1−γ)2
8γ ∥ˆ
A
θold
•
∥∞
M( ˆ
A
θold
•
)
T
(the argument ˆAθold
•
makes the dependence on the advantage explicit),
the per-entry clip bound is Bs,a,τ,t := Tsδ(APC-Obj) −P
(s,a′,τ ′,t′)∈¯
Xs, (a′,τ ′,t′)̸=(a,τ,t) |rs,a′ −1|, and clip(a, B) :=
clip(a, −B+, B+) with B+ := max(B, 0).
We point out that the APC-Obj objective in Definition C.1 removes the (1 −γ)−1 normalization constant in D.4. As the
only factor (Sitting aside δ(APC-Obj)’s dependence on γ, as we eventually will relax it to a constant hyperparameter.
Even if δ(APC−Obj) = 0, it will not make APC-Obj objective diverge) in the discounted APC-Obj objective that
prevents convergence as γ →1 is the multiplier
1
1−γ . This factor is a constant that does not depend on the policy ratios
rs,a, thus removing it does not change the arg max over θ and the APC-Obj policy update, for any value of γ. This
removal corresponds to estimating the discounted objective with respect to the normalized discounted state distribution
¯ρθold
(γ) than the unnormalized one ρθold
(γ) =
1
1−γ ¯ρθold
(γ) . With this convention, the APC-Obj objective converges and is
well-defined at γ = 1. 5
The APC-Obj objective can equivalently be written in the RGF form of Definition 3.1:
Definition C.2 (APC-Obj surrogate objective, in RGF form).
ˆJAPC-Obj(θ|θold) = 1
T
X
(s,a,I)∈E

clip

rs,a −1,

Tsδ(APC-Obj) −
X
(s,a′,I′)∈E
(a′,I′)̸=(a,I),
|rs,a′ −1|

ˆAθold
s,a
+ ˆAθold
s,a

,
(58)
5We also mention that the APC-Obj objective varies smoothly with γ, as the per-state discount weight
Γ(s)
Γ
(Γ(s) =
P
(s′,a,τ,t)∈¯
X, s′=s γt and Γ = P
s Γ(s)) in APC-Obj algorithm varies smoothly with γ. When γ = 1, γt = 1 for the sum-
mand, so Γ(s) = Ts and Γ = T, giving Γ(s)
Γ
= Ts
T for every state. Adding
1
Ts weight for each token given by the APC-Obj in D.4,
one recovering the normalized counting measure over all sampled tokens, the form displayed in Definition C.1 here. Thus removing
of the (1 −γ)−1 factor smoothly and analytically extends the APC-Obj objective to γ = 1 without any discontinuity or singularity,
resulting the exact form in Definition C.1.
27


--- Page 28 ---
where I = (τ, t) carries the trajectory membership and time step associated with state–action pair (s, a) during
sampling, E ⊆X × Tjθold × N is the augmented index set in which each sampled token appears as a separate entry,
and µs,a,I = 1
T .
Reading off the ratio gating map from (58):
Definition C.3 (APC-Obj ratio gating map). The APC-Obj ratio gating map GAPC-Obj : RE
>0 →RE
>0 is
GAPC-Obj(r•)s,a,I = clip

rs,a −1,

Tsδ(APC-Obj) −
X
(s,a′,I′)∈E
(a′,I′)̸=(a,I)
|rs,a′ −1|

+ 1.
(59)
Unlike PPO and GRPO, the APC-Obj gating map depends on the entire ratio tuple r• through the cross-action coupling
in the clip bound, which is what enforces the per-state total-variation constraint in aggregate.
C.2
APC-Obj Budget Consumption and Clipping Regimes
The APC-Obj gating map (Definition C.3) contains a policy-ratio-dependent budget inside the clipping term that
enforces the per-state trust-region constraint. To understand how this budget shapes the gated ratios, we analyze the
per-token clipping behavior by decomposing it into three regimes according to budget consumption.
Budget and consumption terms.
Consider a single per-token objective term from (58):

clip

rs,a −1, Tsδ(APC-Obj)
|
{z
}
(B): budget
−
X
(s,a′,I′)∈E
(a′,I′)̸=(a,I)
|rs,a′ −1|

+ 1

ˆAθold
s,a .
(60)
We identify three components that govern the clipping behavior:
• (B) The total budget: Ts δ(APC-Obj).
• (C1) The cross-token consumption:
X
(s,a′,I′)∈E, (a′,I′)̸=(a,I)
|rs,a′ −1| = Ts ˆDTV(s) −|rs,a −1|, the total
budget already consumed by all other tokens (except a) at state s.
• (C2) The current-token deviation: |rs,a −1|, the amount consumed by the token under consideration.
The sum (C1) + (C2) = Ts ˆDTV(s) is the total per-state TV consumption. The effective clip bound for the current
token is (B) −(C1), and the clipping activates when (C2) exceeds this residual budget.
Three clipping regimes.
The clipping behavior at each token (s, a, I) falls into exactly one of three regimes,
determined by the relationship between the budget (B) and the consumption terms (C1), (C2):
(P) Pass-through regime (clip inactive). The clip acts as the identity, so the gated ratio equals the ungated ratio.
This occurs when
|rs,a −1| ≤Ts δ(APC-Obj) −(C1),
(61)
which, after substituting (C1) + (C2) = Ts ˆDTV(s), reduces to a uniform condition across tokens:
ˆDTV(s) ≤δ(APC-Obj).
In this regime, the aggregate consumption (C1) + (C2) does not exceed the total budget (B), and the per-token
objective coincides with the linear surrogate.
(R) Rollback regime (clip active, output nonzero). The clip is active and suppresses |rs,a −1| to a smaller value,
but does not zero it out. This requires both
ˆDTV(s) > δ(APC-Obj)
and
|rs,a −1|
Ts
> ˆDTV(s) −δ(APC-Obj).
(62)
The first condition is the same uniform budget violation as in (P) (with reversed inequality). The second
condition is token-specific: the cross-token consumption (C1) alone has not exhausted the entire budget, so the
residual clip bound (B) −(C1) > 0 provides a nonzero but reduced clip output. In terms of the clip operator:
0 < | clip(rs,a −1, (B) −(C1))| < |rs,a −1|.
28


--- Page 29 ---
(Z) Zeroed regime (clip output is zero). The clip output is exactly zero, and the token no longer contributes to the
surrogate objective. This occurs when
ˆDTV(s) > δ(APC-Obj)
and
|rs,a −1|
Ts
≤ˆDTV(s) −δ(APC-Obj).
(63)
Here the cross-token consumption (C1) alone already exceeds the total budget (B), making the residual clip
bound (B) −(C1) ≤0, which forces the clip output to zero and eliminates the token’s contribution entirely.
Connection to the retraction property.
By Lemma D.9, whenever the policy ratios enter either the rollback regime (R)
or the zeroed regime (Z)—that is, whenever the per-state TV divergence exceeds the trust-region radius δ(APC-Obj)—the
APC-Obj clipping mechanism suppresses the gated ratios’ per-state TV divergence strictly below δ(APC-Obj). This
ensures that the arg max solution, i.e. the policy update, does not lie outside the trust region BTV-TRPO
δ(APC-Obj).
Remark C.4 (From per-state regimes to aggregate regimes in FiberPO). The three APC-Obj clipping regimes (P), (R),
(Z) are defined at the per-state level with total budget Ts δ(APC-Obj). After trajectory-level aggregation (replacing the per-
state count Ts with the per-trajectory length Tτ; see Appendix E.5.3), logarithmic approximation (rs,a −1 →log rs,a;
see Appendix E.5.2), and clipping decomposition into the fiber bundle gating form (Eq. 77), these three per-state
regimes lift to the three regimes of the aggregate gating function gagg (Eq. 10): pass-through, rollback, and zeroed,
respectively.
C.3
LLM-to-RL Notation Translation
To express PPO, GRPO, and GSPO in RGF form, we first establish the correspondence between the LLM indexing
convention (g, j, i) and the RL notation (τ, t) used throughout this paper.
Index correspondence.
In LLM notation, g indexes the query (prompt), j indexes the response (completion) generated
for that query, and i indexes the token position within the response. The RL counterparts are the initial state s0(τ), the
trajectory τ, and the time step t:
g := s0(τ),
j := τ,
i := t.
Consequently, any per-token quantity qst(τ),at(τ) in RL notation corresponds to q(g)
j,i in LLM notation.
Aggregate ratios.
The positive/negative sequence aggregate ratios s±
τ in RL notation are denoted s(g)±
j
in LLM
notation.
C.4
PPO, GRPO, and GSPO in RGF Form
We give the explicit RGF specifications (E, µ, G) for each method. Recall the general RGF form (Definition 3.1):
ˆJ(θ|θold) =
X
(s,a,I)∈E
µs,a,I G(r•)s,a,I ˆAs,a.
PPO [2].
I = ∅(no extra information), E ≃X, µs,a = µX ({(s, a)}) =
ns,a
T
(normalized counting measure
of state-action pair (s, a), counting appearance multiplicity from all sampled trajectories. T is number of sampled
state-action pairs(counting multiplicity) and ns,a is the multiplicity for state-action pair (s, a). Definition is also given
in Appendix A.2), and
GPPO(r•)s,a = sign( ˆAs,a) · min
␀clip(rs,a, 1 ± ϵ) sign( ˆAs,a), rs,a sign( ˆAs,a)

.
GRPO [3].
I = τ (trajectory membership), E ⊆X × Tjθold, µs,a,τ =
1
|Tjθold| ·
1
Tτ (where Tτ is the total number of
steps within trajectory τ), and GGRPO = GPPO (the same token-wise clipping, but with trajectory-normalized weights).
GSPO [7].
I = τ (trajectory membership), E ⊆X × Tjθold, µs,a,τ =
1
|Tjθold| ·
1
Tτ , and
GGSPO(r•)s,a,τ = lτ · min
␀clip(sτ, 1 ± ϵ) lτ, sτ lτ

,
where Tτ is the total number of steps within trajectory τ, lτ
:=
sign
␀PTτ −1
t=0
ˆAθold
st(τ),at(τ)

, and sτ
:=
␀QTτ −1
t=0
rst(τ),at(τ)
1/Tτ is the geometric-mean aggregate ratio over trajectory τ. Note that GSPO gates all tokens in a
trajectory by the same aggregate sτ, moving the clipping from the token level to the trajectory level.
29


--- Page 30 ---
Remark C.5. If we remove the minimize function from the clippings (i.e., let G(r•)s,a,I = clip(rs,a, 1 ± ϵ) for
PPO/GRPO, and G(r•)s,a,I = clip(sτ, 1 ± ϵ) for GSPO), then the gating functions contain no sign functions (sign and
lτ).
C.5
Detailed Derivation: From APC-Obj to PPO, GRPO, and GSPO
We now trace the precise steps that transform the APC-Obj objective into each of PPO, GRPO, and GSPO, identifying
the relaxation or approximation heuristic at each step. Throughout, rs,a implicitly represents rs,a(θ|θold). We present
the derivations without the minimize function, as it is not derived from trust-region theory but from other considerations
(e.g., preventing excessive reward for actions that happen to have high ratios).
Recall the APC-Obj RGF form (58):
ˆJAPC-Obj(θ|θold) = 1
T
X
(s,a,I)∈E

clip

rs,a −1,

Tsδ(APC-Obj) −
X
(s,a′,I′)∈E
(a′,I′)̸=(a,I),
|rs,a′ −1|

ˆAθold
s,a
+ ˆAθold
s,a

.
(64)
C.5.1
APC-Obj to PPO
The RGF form of PPO (without the minimize function) is:
ˆJPPO(θ|θold) =
X
(s,a)∈X
µs,a clip(rs,a, 1 ± ϵ(PPO)) ˆAs,a.
Step 1 (δ-relaxation).
Replace the vanishing δ(APC-Obj) (which is zero at γ = 1) with a positive tunable hyperparam-
eter δ(PPO); the exact trust-region guarantee is traded for a controllable approximation whose departure is quantified by
δ(PPO) itself. The relaxed objective becomes:
ˆJ(θ|θold) = 1
T
X
(s,a,τ,t)∈¯
X
h
clip

rs,a −1, ±
␀Tsδ(PPO) −
X
(s,a′,τ ′,t′)∈¯
Xs
(a′,τ ′,t′)̸=(a,τ,t)
|rs,a′ −1|

+ 1
i
ˆAθold
s,a .
Step 2 (decoupling).
Decouple the clipping of individual ratios from other ratios by dropping the cross-entry sum
from the clip bound and replacing it with a per-token constant ϵ(PPO). This yields the PPO objective:
ˆJPPO(θ|θold) =
X
(s,a)∈X
µs,a clip(rs,a, 1 ± ϵ(PPO)) ˆAθold
s,a .
This step eliminates the gradient coupling between ratios of different state-action pairs, improving stability and
robustness.
C.5.2
APC-Obj to GRPO
The RGF form of GRPO (without the minimize function) is:
ˆJGRPO(θ|θold) =
X
τ∈Tjθold
Tτ −1
X
t=0
1
|Tjθold|
· 1
Tτ
clip(rs,a, 1 ± ϵ(GRPO)) ˆAs,a,
where s ≡st(τ), a ≡at(τ). The derivation uses the same first two steps as PPO:
Step 1 (δ-relaxation).
δ(APC-Obj) 7→δ(GRPO).
Step 2 (decoupling).
Replace the per-entry clip bound Bs,a,τ,t := Tsδ(APC-Obj) −P
(s,a′,τ ′,t′)∈¯
Xs,
(a′,τ ′,t′)̸=(a,τ,t)
|rs,a′ −1| with
a per-token constant ϵ(GRPO).
30


--- Page 31 ---
Step 3 (sequence length weighting).
Change the sampling weight from the normalized counting measure µs,a to the
trajectory-normalized measure µ(GRPO)({(st(τ), at(τ), τ)}) :=
1
|Tjθold| ·
1
Tτ . This yields the final GRPO objective:
ˆJGRPO(θ|θold) =
X
τ∈Tjθold

· 1

clip(rs,a −1, ±ϵ(GRPO)) ˆAs,a + ˆAs,a.
Compared with PPO, GRPO penalizes extremely long sequences/trajectories via the 1/Tτ normalization.

The RGF form of GSPO (without the minimize function) is:

Step 1 (δ-relaxation).

It is also worth mentioning that in GSPO, the advantage estimation is chosen so that ˆAs,a agrees across all tokens within
the same trajectory, so we can denote ˆAτ as the common advantage for trajectory τ. If the advantage estimation is not
chosen in this way simply denote ˆAτ as the average advantage across all tokens within trajectory τ.
Step 3 (log-ratio approximation).
Replace r −1 by log r, which provides better algebraic properties. Recall

Inspecting the clipping term, one sees that it activates only when Tτ ˆ¯D(τ)

therefore consider two cases:
1. Tτ ˆ¯D(τ)

seq
: clipping applies to all individual log policy ratios within the trajectory.
2. Tτ ˆ¯D(τ)

seq
: all clipping functions reduce to the identity.

ˆJCase (2)(θ|θold) =
X

· (log sτ + 1) ˆAτ.

Step 4 (clip-bound approximation).

≥Tτ ˆ¯D(τ)
TV. By Pinsker’s in-

Furthermore, GSPO implicitly requires the gradient to vanish when case (1) holds. Using this, we can combine both
cases using the bound we just obtained via a simple clipping function on the aggregated ratio:
ˆJ(θ|θold) =
1

C.6
Proof of Claim 3.5: GSPO Maintains a trajectory-level TV distance bound
Proof. For notation, see: Appendix A.1. Recall the GSPO objective function in RGF form (without minimization
clipping):
ˆJGSPO(θ|θold) =
X
τ∈Tjθold
Tτ −1
X
t=0
(s≡st(τ), a≡at(τ))
1
|Tjθold|
· 1
Tτ
clip(sτ, 1 ± ϵ(GSPO)) ˆAs,a.
We write ϵ instead of ϵ(GSPO) for convenience. Given a sampled trajectory τ, the log-aggregate ratio is defined to be
log sτ :=
1
Tτ
PTτ −1
t=0
log rst(τ),at(τ). This is a Monte Carlo estimation for the negative trajectory-normalized average
KL divergence (at the trajectory level), see (49). Thus, the clipping of the aggregate ratio in the objective function
maintains
¯D(tj)
KL
(50)
≈ˆ¯D(τ)
KL ≤−log(1 −ϵ) ≈ϵ
(when ϵ ∼0).
Furthermore, based on 1
2DKL ≥(DTV)2 (Pinsker’s inequality) and Ep[X2] ≥Ep[X]2 for probability measures, we
have:
␀ ¯D(tj)
TV
2 = Es∼¯ρ
θold
(tj) [DTV(s)]2 ≤Es∼¯ρ
θold
(tj) [DTV(s)2] ≤Es∼¯ρ
θold
(tj)
 1
2DKL(s)

=
1
2 ¯D(tj)
KL ≲
ϵ
2.
Therefore ¯D(tj)
TV ≲
p
ϵ/2, which provides a rough estimation for δ which also bounds the trajectory-normalized average
TV divergence. We roughly conclude that δ ∼
p
ϵ/2.
D
Equivalence of APC-Obj and sample-based TV-TRPO
This appendix establishes that the Aggregational Policy Censoring Objective (APC-Obj) algorithm and sample-based
Total Variation Trust Region Policy Optimization (TV-TRPO) produce identical policy updates under standard function
approximation assumptions. The equivalence is nontrivial because APC-Obj and sample-based TV-TRPO have
superficially different structures: APC-Obj applies per-token clipping with cross-action coupling, while sample-based
TV-TRPO maximizes a globally penalized objective. The proof proceeds in three stages: (i) we characterize the
maximizer of sample-based TV-TRPO (Theorem D.3), (ii) we show that APC-Obj’s clipping mechanism implicitly
enforces the same trust-region constraint (Lemma D.9), and (iii) we unite the two by showing both produce the same
policy ratio deviation (Theorem D.10).
32


--- Page 33 ---
D.1
Sampling Notation and Standing Assumptions
Let X denote the set of all state–action pairs encountered in the sampled trajectories, and let ns,a denote the sample
multiplicity of pair (s, a) in the sampled set. Write S[X] for the set of all states appearing in X, and for each state
s ∈S[X], let Xs := {a ∈A | (s, a) ∈X} be the set of actions co-occurring with s. Define T := P
(s,a)∈X ns,a as
the total sample count (with multiplicity), and Ts := P
a∈Xs ns,a as the per-state sample count. We also denote the
augmented state–action pair space ¯
X := {(s, a, τ, t) | (s, a) ∈X, τ ∈Tjθold, t ∈{0, . . . , Tτ −1}}, the set of all
sampled state–action pairs augmented with their trajectory membership τ and trajectory time step t. Each element of ¯
X
is a distinct sampled token, so | ¯
X| = T. The multiplicity ns,a counts how many augmented entries in ¯
X share the pair
(s, a): ns,a = |{(τ, t) | (s, a, τ, t) ∈¯
X}|.
The policy importance-sampling ratio is rs,a(θ|θold) := πθ(a|s) / πθold(a|s), and we write the ratio deviation as
∆s,a := rs,a −1.
We adopt two standing identities. Both hold exactly for any policy πθ; their sample-based counterparts are approxima-
tions that we assume hold exactly throughout this section.
1. Probability constraint. Since P
a∈A πθ(a|s) = 1 for any policy πθ (not only πθold), the identity
Ea∼πθold(·|s)[rs,a] =
X
a∈A
πθold(a|s) πθ(a|s)
πθold(a|s) =
X
a∈A
πθ(a|s) = 1
holds for every θ; the πθold factors cancel algebraically, so the identity does not require πθ = πθold. The
sample-based counterpart replaces the expectation with an average over the observed action set Xs:
1
Ts
X
a∈Xs
ns,a rs,a = 1
⇐⇒
X
a∈Xs
ns,a ∆s,a = 0,
∀s ∈S[X].
(65)
This approximation incurs error from two sources: (i) actions in A \ Xs where πθ may place mass but no
samples exist, and (ii) finite-sample noise in the empirical frequencies ns,a/Ts. We assume both errors are
negligible for the purposes of this analysis.
2. Advantage centering. By definition of the advantage, Ea∼πθold(·|s)[Aθold
s,a ] = 0, so under sampling:
1
Ts
X
a∈Xs
ns,a ˆAθold
s,a
= 0,
∀s ∈S[X].
(66)
We assume both sample-based identities hold exactly throughout this section.
Discounted estimators.
In the discounted case, there are a few consistent estimators for discounted quantities. For
any X : S × A →R,
Eτ∼Pθ
hTτ −1
X
t=0
γtXst(τ),at(τ)
i
= E(s,a)∼ρθ
(γ)·πθ[Xs,a] =
1
1 −γ E(s,a)∼¯ρθ
(γ)·πθ[Xs,a] =
1
1 −γ Es∼¯ρθ
(γ)[Ea∼πθ(·|s)[Xs,a]]
(the second from the definition of the normalized discounted distribution).
The discounted naive Monte Carlo estimator is
ˆEτ∼Pθ
hTτ −1
X
t=0
γtXst(τ),at(τ)
i
=
1
|Tjθ|
X
τ∈Tjθ
Tτ −1
X
t=0
γtXst(τ),at(τ).
An alternative estimator leverages the time independence of the policy πθ(a|s):
1
1 −γ
ˆE(s,a)∼¯ρθ
(γ)·πθ[Xs,a] =
1
1 −γ
X
s∈S[X]
Γ(s)
Γ
 
1
Ts
X
a∈Xs
ns,aXs,a
!
,
where Γ(s) := P
(s′,a,τ,t)∈¯
X, s′=s γt and Γ = P
s∈S[X] Γ(s). It is worth noting that Γ(s)/Γ ∼¯ρθ
(γ)(s) when number
of samples T →∞.
We choose this estimator as our discounted-case sampling estimator, for the following reason: no matter at which step
a state st = s is encountered, the action at ∼πθ(·|st) is always a valid sample from πθ(·|s) and contributes equally
to the estimate of Ea∼πθ(·|st)[Xst,a], because the policy is always time-independent. However, the naive discounted
Monte Carlo estimator always weights this (st, at) by γt, reducing the contribution of valid policy samples drawn at
large time steps and thereby wasting their potential for better estimation.
33


--- Page 34 ---
D.2
The Sample-Based TV-TRPO Objective
The sample-based TV-TRPO objective is the Monte Carlo counterpart of the penalized surrogate (56).

of the sample-based TV-TRPO objective, obtained by replacing the expectations with sample averages:

When multiple maximizers exist, sample-based TV-TRPO selects the one with smallest ratio-deviation L2 norm:
min P

a∈Xs |rs,a −1|2.
Note the second term contains the per-state estimated TV divergence ˆDTV(s) (Eq. 39), the Monte-Carlo estimator of
the per-state TV distance DTV(s) (Eq. 34); see Appendix A.3.1 for the full definitions and ratio forms. The max-TV
penalty couples all actions at every state, making the penalized objective a quadratic function of max per-state TV
divergence ˆDmax
TV = maxs∈S[X] ˆDTV(s).
Reformulation in ratio deviations.

D.3
Characterizing the Sample-Based TV-TRPO Maximizer
The key insight is that TV-TRPO’s quadratic penalty on max TV divergence forces the optimal solution to equalize the
TV divergence across all states. This section makes this statement precise.
Per-state normalized-TV maximizers.
For each state s, consider maximizing the following auxiliary objective over
the ratio deviations:
ˆ∆∗
s,• := arg
max
∆s,•: ˆ
DTV(s,∆)=1,
P
a∈Xs ns,a ∆s,a=0
X
a∈Xs
ns,a ∆s,a ˆAθold
s,a ,
(69)
with two constraints:
(i) the estimated per-state TV divergence
ˆDTV(s, ∆)
=
1
Ts
P
a∈Xs ns,a |∆s,a|
=
1
Ts
P
a∈Xs ns,a |rs,a −1| is fixed to 1, and (ii) the probability constraint P
a∈Xs ns,a ∆s,a = 0.
Let M(s) := P
a∈Xs ns,a ˆ∆∗
s,a ˆAθold
s,a denote the optimal objective value of this per-state problem.
Linearity of TV maximizer.
Because the objective is linear in ∆s,•, the TV divergence constraint ˆDTV(s, ∆) = c
is also linear in |∆s,•|, and the probability constraint P
a ns,a ∆s,a = 0 is linear, the maximizer at radius c is simply
c ˆ∆∗
s,•, with optimal value c M(s):
c ˆ∆∗
s,• = arg
max
∆s,•: ˆ
DTV(s,∆)=c,
P
a ns,a∆s,a=0
X
a∈Xs
ns,a ∆s,a ˆAθold
s,a
= c M(s),
c ≥0.
(70)
The same conclusion holds when the equality constraint is relaxed to ˆDTV(s, ∆) ≤c: Writing ∆s,• = c∗ˆ∆s,• with
c∗≤c and ˆDTV(s, ˆ∆) = 1, one can reduce the problem to maxc∗≤c c∗M(s), which is attained at c∗= c:
arg
max
∆s,•: ˆ
DTV(s,∆)≤c,
P
a∈Xs ns,a ∆s,a=0
X
a∈Xs
ns,a ∆s,a ˆAθold
s,a
=
arg
max
c∗ˆ∆s,•: c∗≤c, ˆ

P
a∈Xs ns,a ˆ∆s,a=0



s,•.

Theorem D.3 (Sample-based TV-TRPO maximizer). Suppose ∆∗
• maximizes j(∆) (equivalently, ˆJ(TV-TRPO)) subject
to the probability constraint (65). Then
∆∗
s,a = t∗ˆ∆∗
s,a,
t∗=
1 −γ
8γ ∥ˆAθold
•
∥∞
M.
35


--- Page 36 ---
Proof. Let t := ˆDmax
TV (∆∗) = maxs∈S[X] ˆDTV(s, ∆∗).
We first establish that ˆDTV(s, ∆∗) = t for every s ∈S[X]. Note that if M(s) = 0 for every state s, then M = 0,
t∗= 0, and max j(∆) = 0, so the theorem holds trivially with ∆∗= 0. We may therefore assume M(s) > 0 for at
least one state.
Suppose for contradiction that some state s with M(s) > 0 has ˆDTV(s, ∆∗) = t(s) < t. Since ∆∗maximizes j,
the per-state component ∆∗
s,• must maximize P
a ns,a ∆s,a ˆAθold
s,a subject to ˆDTV(s, ∆) = t(s) and the probability
constraint. By the maximizer linearity (70), ∆∗
s,• = t(s) ˆ∆∗
s,•, achieving value t(s) M(s).
Now construct ∆∗∗by setting ∆∗∗
s,• := t(s)+t
2
ˆ∆∗
s,• while keeping ∆∗∗
s′,• = ∆∗
s′,• for all s′ ̸= s. The per-state advantage
contribution at s increases to t(s)+t
2
M(s) > t(s) M(s), while ˆDmax
TV (∆∗∗) = t since ˆDTV(s, ∆∗∗) = t(s)+t
2
< t.
Therefore:
j(∆∗∗) =
1
(1−γ)Γ
X
s∈S[X]
Γ(s)
Ts
X
a∈Xs
ns,a ∆∗∗
s,a ˆAθold
s,a
−4γ ∥ˆAθold
•
∥∞
(1 −γ)2
t2
=
Γ(s)
(1 −γ)Γ

2Ts
M(s) +
1
(1−γ)Γ

s∈S[X]
Γ(s)
Ts
X
a∈Xs
ns,a ∆∗

−4γ ∥ˆAθold

∥∞

(1 −γ)Γ
t −t(s)
2Ts
M(s) + j(∆∗) > j(∆∗),
where the strict inequality holds since M(s) > 0 and t > t(s). This contradicts the optimality of ∆∗. For states with
M(s) = 0, the per-state advantage contribution is zero(by TV-TRPO algorithm, when multiple argmax exists, the
smallest L2 norm solution is chosen, thus forces ˆDTV(s) = 0), thus we can simply not consider them without loss of
generality. Hence ˆDTV(s, ∆∗) = t for all s (except those with M(s) = 0).
Since ˆDTV(s, ∆∗) = t for every s, and each ∆∗
s,• must maximize its per-state advantage contribution subject to
this constraint, the maximizer linearity (70) gives ∆∗
s,• = t ˆ∆∗
s,• for all s. The discount-weighted total advantage
contribution is then:
1
Γ
X
s∈S[X]
Γ(s)
Ts
X

ns,a ∆∗
s,a ˆAθold
s,a
=

Γ

s∈S[X]

Ts
M(s) = t M.
Substituting into j:
j(∆∗) =
t M

∥∞

t2.
This is a concave quadratic in t with maximum at

8γ ∥ˆAθold

D.4
The APC-Obj Objective (In discounted case)
Definition D.4 (APC-Obj policy iteration,
in discounted case). The APC-Obj update selects θnew
=

ˆJAPC-Obj(θ|θold) =

a∈Xs

−
X
(s,a′,τ ′,t′)∈¯
X
(a′,τ ′,t′)̸=(a,τ,t)
|rs,a′ −1|

ˆAθold
s,a + ˆAθold
s,a
i
,
(72)
where δ(APC-Obj) :=
1−γ
8γ ∥ˆ

θold
•
∥∞M, and M is also used in the sample-based TV-TRPO maximizer (Theorem D.3).
The per-entry clip bound is
Bs,a,τ,t := Tsδ(APC-Obj) −
X
(s,a′,τ ′,t′)∈¯
X, (a′,τ ′,t′)̸=(a,τ,t)
|rs,a′ −1|,
36


--- Page 37 ---
and clip(a, B) := clip(a, −B+, B+) with B+ := max(B, 0). When multiple argmax exists, we assume APC-Obj
chooses the one with smallest ratio-deviation L2 norm.
D.5
Surrogated APC-Obj Objective and Objective Equivalence
To analyze APC-Obj, we introduce a surrogate formulation that replaces the policy parameter θ with the ratio deviation
∆directly.
Definition D.5 (Surrogated APC-Obj objective). For ratio deviations ∆• and threshold δ, define
j(APC-Obj)(∆, δ) :=
1
(1−γ)Γ
X
s∈S[X]
Γ(s)
Ts
X
a∈Xs
h
ns,a clip

∆s,a, Tsδ −
X
a′∈Xs
(ns,a′ −δa,a′) |∆s,a′|

ˆAθold
s,a
+ ns,a(1 −C(∆)s) ˆAθold
s,a
i
,
(73)
where δa,a′ is the Kronecker delta, and the correction term is
C(∆)s :=
1
Ts
X
a∈Xs
ns,a clip

∆s,a, Tsδ(APC-Obj) −
X
a′∈Xs
(ns,a′ −δa,a′) |∆s,a′|

.
(74)
The correction term C(∆)s vanishes whenever ˆDTV(s, ∆) < δ(APC-Obj): in that regime, the clipping is inactive (all
clip terms are identities), and the probability constraint (65) gives C(∆)s =
1
Ts
P
a ns,a ∆s,a = 0.
Lemma D.6 (Objective equivalence). Let ∆•(θ) := r•(θ|θold) −1. Then
j(APC-Obj)(∆(θ), δ(APC-Obj)) = ˆJAPC-Obj(θ|θold).
Proof. Expanding the surrogated objective and collecting terms:
j(APC-Obj)(∆(θ), δ(APC-Obj)) = ˆJAPC-Obj(θ|θold) −
1
(1−γ)Γ
X
s∈S[X]
Γ(s)
Ts
X
a∈Xs
ns,a C(∆(θ))s ˆAθold
s,a
= ˆJAPC-Obj(θ|θold) −
1
(1 −γ)Γ
X
s∈S[X]
Γ(s) C(∆(θ))s · 1
Ts
X
a∈Xs
ns,a ˆAθold
s,a .
The advantage centering identity (66) ensures
1
Ts
P
a∈Xs ns,a ˆAθold
s,a = 0 for each s, so the correction vanishes.
Remark D.7 (Equivalence with the original form using ¯X). The surrogated form is obtained from the orig-
inal APC-Obj (Definition D.4) by grouping entries that share the same pair (s, a) and this extra constant
1
(1−γ)Γ
P
s∈S[X]
Γ(s)C(∆)s
Ts
P
a∈Xs ns,a ∆s,a, term that evaluates to 0. The augmented state-action pairs (s, a, τ, t) in
the space ¯
X with the same (s, a) has the same ratio rs,a and the same clip bound. Collecting the ns,a identical terms
produces ns,a clip(∆s,a, Tsδ −P
a′(ns,a′ −δa,a′)|∆s,a′|).
D.6
Retraction Property of APC-Obj Clipping
The following lemma establishes that the APC-Obj clipping mechanism acts as a retraction: when a candidate solution
violates the TV constraint, the clipping projects the gated ratios back strictly inside the trust region. Combining this
retraction property with the property that smaller ˆDTV(s) always produce argmax solutions with less objective value,
the next theorem D.10 used these two properties to show APC-Obj implicitly enforces the update policy, given by the
argmax, lies within TV trust-region constraint. Thus, this property can be seen as the mechanism by which APC-Obj
enforces the trust-region constraint, without explicitly constraining the optimization problem.
Definition D.8 (Clipping operator and centered projection). For any vector us,a ∈RX satisfying P
a∈Xs ns,a us,a = 0
for all s ∈S[X], define the clipping operator
G(u•)s,a := clip
␀us,a, Ts(δ −ˆDTV(s, u)) + |us,a|

,
and the centered projection
proj(G(u))s,a := G(u)s,a −C(G(u))s.
Lemma D.9 (Retraction property). For any u• satisfying the probability constraint (65):
37


--- Page 38 ---
1. ˆDTV
␀s, proj(G(u))

≤δ for all s.
2. If ˆDTV(s, u) > δ, then ˆDTV
␀s, proj(G(u))

< δ (strict inequality).
Proof. When ˆDTV(s, u) ≤δ, the clipping is inactive, proj(G(u)) = u, and the claim holds trivially. It remains to
prove the case for ˆDTV(s, u) > δ. Since all expressions aggregate only over Xs ⊆A, we fix a state s without loss of
generality.
For any vector wa ∈RXs, we have Ts ˆDTV(s, w) = P
a∈Xs ns,a |wa|.
Write va := G(u)s,a and ¯v :=
1
Ts
P
a∈Xs ns,a va; by definition, ¯v = C(u)s. Observe that G(−w) = −G(w), C(−w) = −C(w), and ˆDTV(s, −w) =
ˆDTV(s, w); hence proving the result for u implies it holding for −u as well. Thus we may therefore proceed to prove
only the ¯v ≥0 case without loss of generality: if ¯v < 0, prove with −u, so that (−u) = C(−u) = −¯v > 0, and by
proving the result for −u, the above symmetric property implies it holding for u that has ¯v < 0 as well.
Let X +
s (w) := {a ∈Xs | wa > 0}. By definition of ¯v, P
a∈Xs ns,a(va −¯v) = 0. Splitting the absolute value sum into
its positive and negative parts:
Ts ˆDTV(s, v −¯v) =
X
a∈Xs
ns,a |va −¯v|
=
X
a∈X +
s (v−¯v)
ns,a(va −¯v) −
X
a∈X +
s (¯v−v)
ns,a(va −¯v).
Since
␀P
a∈X +
s (v−¯v) ns,a(va −¯v)

+
␀P
a∈X +
s (¯v−v) ns,a(va −¯v)

= P
a∈Xs ns,a(va −¯v) = 0, the two partial sums
are equal in magnitude, giving:
Ts ˆDTV(s, v −¯v) = 2
X
a∈X +
s (v−¯v)
ns,a(va −¯v).
Since ¯v ≥0, every a ∈X +
s (v −¯v) satisfies va > 0, which implies us,a > 0 (the clipping va = G(u)s,a preserves the
sign of us,a whenever va ̸= 0). When ˆDTV(s, u) > δ, the clipping reduces the absolute value of us,a to va by exactly
Ts( ˆDTV(s, u) −δ) for va > 0, i.e.:
us,a = va + Ts( ˆDTV(s, u) −δ) > va.
Substituting va = us,a −Ts( ˆDTV(s, u) −δ) for each a ∈X +
s (v −¯v):
Ts ˆDTV(s, v −¯v) = 2
X
a∈X +
s (v−¯v)
ns,a(va −¯v)
= 2
X
a∈X +
s (v−¯v)
ns,a
␀us,a −Ts( ˆDTV(s, u) −δ) −¯v

= 2
X
a∈X +
s (v−¯v)
ns,a us,a −2
X
a∈X +
s (v−¯v)
ns,a
␀Ts( ˆDTV(s, u) −δ) + ¯v

.
The chain of inequalities us,a > va ≥va −¯v gives X +
s (v −¯v) ⊆X +
s (v) ⊆X +
s (u). The probability constraint yields
P
a∈X +
s (u) ns,a us,a = P
a∈X +
s (−u) ns,a (−us,a) (from P
a ns,a us,a = 0) and consequently 2 P
a∈X +
s (u) ns,a us,a =
Ts ˆDTV(s, u). Combining these:
Ts ˆDTV(s, v −¯v) ≤2
X
a∈X +
s (u)
ns,a us,a −2
X
a∈X +
s (v−¯v)
ns,a
␀Ts( ˆDTV(s, u) −δ) + ¯v

= Ts ˆDTV(s, u) −N
␀Ts( ˆDTV(s, u) −δ) + ¯v

= TsNδ −Ts(N −1) ˆDTV(s, u) −N¯v
= Tsδ −Ts(N −1)
␀ ˆDTV(s, u) −δ

−N¯v,
where N := 2 P
a∈X +
s (v−¯v) ns,a ∈N is necessarily even (being twice a sum of positive integers).
38


--- Page 39 ---
Noting that proj(G(u)) = v −¯v, two cases arise. If N = 0, then va ≤¯v for all a, so ˆDTV(s, proj(G(u))) = 0 < δ
(since P
a ns,a(va −¯v) = 0 forces va = ¯v for all a). If N ≥2 (since N is even and positive), then Ts(N −1) ≥
Ts ≥1 > 0. Combined with ˆDTV(s, u) −δ > 0 and N¯v ≥0, this gives Ts(N −1)( ˆDTV(s, u) −δ) + N¯v > 0, and
therefore Ts ˆDTV(s, proj(G(u))) < Tsδ, i.e., ˆDTV(s, proj(G(u))) < δ. In either case, the strict inequality holds.
D.7
Main Theorem: Sample-Based TV-TRPO and APC-Obj Equivalence
Theorem D.10 (Sample-based TV-TRPO APC-Obj equivalence). Suppose θ satisfies standard function approximation
assumptions, then APC-Obj and sample-based TV-TRPO produce the same policy update:
πθ(APC-Obj)
new
= πθ(TV-TRPO)
new
.
Proof. By Lemma D.6, optimizing the APC-Obj objective over θ is equivalent to optimizing the surrogated APC-Obj
objective j(APC-Obj)(∆, δ(APC-Obj)) over ratio deviations ∆. We therefore work with j(APC-Obj) directly.

The APC-Obj maximizer satisfies the trust-region constraint. Let ∆∗denote the maximizer of j(APC-Obj)(·, δ(APC-Obj)).

Reduction to constrained linear maximization. Since ˆDTV(s, ∆∗) ≤δ(APC-Obj) for all s, the clipping terms in (75)

By the maximizer linearity (70), the unique maximizer is ∆∗

Identification with the sample-based TV-TRPO maximizer.

Lifting to policies.
Let θ∗denote the policy update that maximizes the surrogated APC-Obj objective
j(APC-Obj)(∆(θ), δ(APC-Obj)).
If ∆(θ∗) did not coincide with ∆∗, then under the standard function approx-
imation assumptions there would exist θ∗∗with ∆(θ∗∗) = ∆∗, giving j(APC−Obj)(∆(θ∗∗), δ(APC-Obj)) >
j(APC−Obj)(∆(θ∗), δ(APC-Obj)), contradicting the optimality of θ∗. Hence ∆(θ∗) = ∆∗= δ(APC-Obj) ˆ∆∗
•.
By Lemma D.6, ∆(θ∗) also maximizes the original APC-Obj objective. By Theorem D.3, ∆(θ∗) = t∗ˆ∆∗
• is also the
TV-TRPO maximizer. Therefore:
πθ(APC-Obj)
new
= (δ(APC-Obj) ˆ∆∗
• + 1) πθold = πθ(TV-TRPO)
new
.
